open-meteo-client
  • Explanation
  • Why async-only
  • View page source

Why async-only

open-meteo-client exposes an async public API and does not ship a synchronous counterpart. Here’s the reasoning.

Async is the dominant style for new I/O code in 2026

  • Modern HTTP libraries (httpx, aiohttp) are async-first.

  • Frameworks that commonly consume weather data (FastAPI, Starlette, Home Assistant) are async throughout.

  • Typed Python has excellent async ergonomics — async def, async with, context managers, cancellation.

Sync is easy to add later if needed

httpx supports both sync and async with nearly identical APIs. If real demand for a sync surface appears, we can add one without changing the async one.

Sync users today have an escape hatch

You can always run the async API from synchronous code:

import asyncio
from openmeteo import ping

asyncio.run_in_executor  # not needed here — ping() is sync

Once today() lands:

import asyncio
from openmeteo import today

forecast = asyncio.run(today("Dresden"))

One line of ceremony per call. Acceptable.

The cost of shipping both

Two surfaces doubles the test matrix, the documentation, and the bug reports. Until someone asks for sync, it’s pure overhead.

Decision

Async-only for v0.1.0. Revisit when either (a) real users ask for sync or (b) we build something (like a CLI) that would benefit from a sync entrypoint.
